back_translation: When Festus had arrived in the province of Judea three days earlier, he traveled from the city of Caesarea up to Jerusalem.
The chief priests and the council of leaders of the Jews went and filed a formal accusation against Paul before him, and they pleaded with him,
asking from him a favor in the matter of Paul, namely to send Paul to Jerusalem—while they themselves were planning to ambush and kill Paul along the way.
Festus therefore replied that Paul was being held in custody at Caesarea, and that he himself was about to return there shortly.
"Therefore," he said, "let those who have authority among you go down with me, and if this man has done anything wrong, let them prosecute him."
When Festus had stayed with them no more than eight or ten days, he went down to the city of Caesarea, and the next day he sat on the judgment seat and ordered Paul to be brought in.
When Paul arrived, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem stood around him and brought many serious charges that they were unable to prove.
Paul made his defense, saying, "I have not committed any wrong, neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar."
But Festus, wanting to do the Jews a favor, answered Paul, "Will you go up to Jerusalem and stand trial before me there on this matter?"
Paul replied, "I am standing before the judgment seat of Caesar, which is the place where I ought to be tried. I have not done anything wrong against the Jews, as you yourself know very well.

If I have done wrong and committed anything worthy of the death penalty, I do not refuse death. But if there is nothing in the charges these people bring against me that is true, then no one has the right to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar."
Festus, after conferring with his council of advisors, replied, "You have appealed to Caesar; to Caesar you shall go."
After several days had passed, King Agrippa and Bernice came to the city of Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus.
While their majesties were staying there for many days, Festus laid out Paul's case before the king, saying, "There is a certain man whom Felix left behind as a prisoner.
When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews filed a formal complaint against him before me, asking that he be condemned.

I replied to them that it is not the custom of the Romans to hand over any person before the accused has met his accusers face-to-face and has had the opportunity to make a defense against the charges.
Therefore when they came together here, I did not delay; the next day I sat on the judgment seat and ordered the man to be brought in.
When his accusers stood up, they did not bring forward any charges concerning serious crimes that I had expected.
Instead they had certain disputes with him concerning their own religion, and concerning a dead man named Jesus, whom Paul affirmed to be alive.
Since I did not know how to investigate these matters, I asked him whether he would be willing to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on this matter.
But when Paul appealed to be held in custody for the decision of His Imperial Majesty, I ordered that he be held until I could send him to Caesar."
Then Agrippa said to Festus, "I myself also would like to hear this man." Festus answered, "Tomorrow Your Majesty shall hear him."

The next day Agrippa and Bernice entered with great ceremonial pomp, together with the commanders and the leading men of that city, and went into the audience hall; then Festus ordered Paul to be brought in.

Festus then said, "King Agrippa and all of you who are seated together with us here, you see this man, about whom the whole multitude of Jews appealed to me both at Jerusalem and here, shouting that he ought not to live any longer.
But I found that he had done nothing worthy of the death penalty, and when he himself appealed to His Imperial Majesty, I decided to send him.

I have nothing definite to write to His Imperial Majesty concerning him; therefore I have brought him before you all, and especially before Your Majesty, King Agrippa, so that when the inquiry has been completed, I may have something to write in my report.
For it seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner without specifying the charges against him.
